summary: Geoffrey Hinton discusses his ethical boundaries regarding AI, specifically his refusal to work on weapons, using the analogy of a backhoe. He also reflects on the history of neural networks, explaining that early struggles with backpropagation in the 1990s were simply a matter of insufficient data and compute scale, rather than a flaw in the algorithm itself.

principles.statement: Never deliberately work on making weapons.
principles.rationale: AI is a powerful tool, much like heavy machinery. Designing it specifically to cause harm is a misuse of the technology.
principles.source_quote: I guess I would never deliberately work on making weapons. I mean, you could design a backhoe that was very good at knocking people's heads off. And I think that would be a bad use of a backhoe, and I wouldn't work on it.

principles.statement: The early failures of backpropagation were a problem of scale, not a flaw in the algorithm.
principles.rationale: In the 1990s, computers were slow and datasets were small, which caused neural networks to get confused by noise compared to other methods. The algorithm just needed massive scale to work.
principles.source_quote: And it was just a question of scale, but we didn't really know that then.

mental_models.name: AI as a Backhoe
mental_models.description: Viewing artificial intelligence as a powerful, dual-use piece of heavy machinery. It can be used constructively to build things, or destructively to cause harm, depending entirely on how it is designed and applied.
mental_models.example: Choosing not to work on AI weapons is like choosing not to design a backhoe optimized for knocking people's heads off.

signature_quotes.text: I guess I would never deliberately work on making weapons. I mean, you could design a backhoe that was very good at knocking people's heads off. And I think that would be a bad use of a backhoe, and I wouldn't work on it.
signature_quotes.context: When asked about the choices he makes regarding the dual-use nature of AI.

signature_quotes.text: So that was very depressing, because in the ’80s we developed back propagation. We thought it would solve everything. And we were a bit puzzled about why it didn't solve everything. And it was just a question of scale, but we didn't really know that then.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the 'AI winter' of the 1990s when neural networks fell out of favor among computer scientists.

anti_patterns.description: Judging the efficacy of neural networks based on their performance on small datasets.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: On small datasets, neural networks get confused by noise, making other methods like support vector machines appear superior. Neural networks require large-scale data and fast compute to demonstrate their actual capability.
